title: How to Learn Spanish for Beginners: A Step-by-Step Roadmap
goal: How to learn Spanish for beginners
metaDescription: How to Learn Spanish for Beginners: A Step-by-Step Roadmap — a free beginner-level guide covering how to learn spanish for beginners. Learn with clear...

roadmap.title: How to Learn Spanish for Beginners: A Step-by-Step Roadmap

modules.title: Spanish Pronunciation and First Words
modules.focus: Master the Spanish alphabet, vowel sounds, and basic greetings to build a strong phonetic foundation.
objectives: Pronounce all Spanish vowels and consonants, including the rolled 'r' and 'ñ'
Understand syllabification and rules for written accents
Introduce yourself and others using basic greetings and farewells
Use polite expressions such as 'por favor' and 'gracias'
modules.description: Master the Spanish alphabet, vowel sounds, and basic greetings to build a strong phonetic foundation.

modules.title: Nouns, Gender, and Numbers
modules.focus: Understand noun gender, pluralization, definite and indefinite articles, and numbers 0-100.
objectives: Identify the grammatical gender of nouns based on their endings
Match definite and indefinite articles to the gender and number of nouns
Form plural nouns correctly
Count from 0 to 100 and understand Spanish number formation rules
modules.description: Understand noun gender, pluralization, definite and indefinite articles, and numbers 0-100.

modules.title: Subject Pronouns and Present Tense Regular Verbs
modules.focus: Conjugate regular -ar, -er, and -ir verbs in the present tense alongside subject pronouns.
objectives: Identify all subject pronouns, understanding the formal/informal distinction for 'you'
Recognize infinitive verb endings and categorize them as -ar, -er, or -ir
Conjugate regular -ar, -er, and -ir verbs in the present tense
Build simple subject-verb sentences to state basic facts and actions
modules.description: Conjugate regular -ar, -er, and -ir verbs in the present tense alongside subject pronouns.

modules.title: Forming Sentences: Questions and Negation
modules.focus: Form simple declarative sentences, ask questions using interrogative words, and apply basic negation.
objectives: Construct simple declarative sentences using standard Spanish word order
Form yes/no questions using rising intonation and inverted question marks
Use common interrogative words (qué, dónde, cuándo, cómo, por qué, quién)
Make sentences negative using 'no' before the verb
modules.description: Form simple declarative sentences, ask questions using interrogative words, and apply basic negation.

modules.title: The Two 'To Be' Verbs: Ser and Estar
modules.focus: Differentiate between the verbs 'ser' and 'estar' to express permanent traits versus temporary states.
objectives: Conjugate the irregular verbs 'ser' and 'estar' in the present tense
Apply the acronym DOCTOR (Description, Occupation, Characteristic, Time, Origin, Relationship) for 'ser'
Apply the acronym PLACE (Position, Location, Action, Condition, Emotion) for 'estar'
Form basic sentences describing people, places, and conditions
modules.description: Differentiate between the verbs 'ser' and 'estar' to express permanent traits versus temporary states.

modules.title: Expressing Possession and Description
modules.focus: Use possessive adjectives and descriptive adjectives while ensuring proper gender and number agreement.
objectives: Identify and use possessive adjectives (mi, tu, su, nuestro, etc.)
Position descriptive adjectives correctly after the noun
Ensure adjectives agree in gender and number with the nouns they modify
Use the verb 'tener' to express age and possession
modules.description: Use possessive adjectives and descriptive adjectives while ensuring proper gender and number agreement.

modules.title: High-Frequency Irregular Verbs
modules.focus: Conjugate and apply high-frequency irregular verbs like 'tener', 'ir', 'hacer', and 'querer'.
objectives: Conjugate common stem-changing verbs (e.g., e>ie, o>ue) in the present tense
Conjugate and use 'ir' to talk about going places
Use 'tener' expressions for physical states (hambre, sed, sueño)
Use 'ir a + infinitive' to construct the near future tense
modules.description: Conjugate and apply high-frequency irregular verbs like 'tener', 'ir', 'hacer', and 'querer'.

modules.title: Expressing Likes and Dislikes with Gustar
modules.focus: Structure sentences using the verb 'gustar' and indirect object pronouns to express preferences.
objectives: Understand the unique sentence structure used with 'gustar'
Identify and use indirect object pronouns (me, te, le, nos, os, les)
Conjugate 'gustar' in the singular and plural based on the object being liked
Apply similar verbs like 'encantar' and 'interesar' to express varying degrees of preference
modules.description: Structure sentences using the verb 'gustar' and indirect object pronouns to express preferences.

modules.title: Telling Time and Daily Routines
modules.focus: Tell time, discuss dates, and describe daily routines using reflexive verbs.
objectives: Ask and tell time using 'ser' and prepositions of time (a, de, hasta)
State the days of the week, months, and seasons
Identify reflexive verbs and their corresponding reflexive pronouns
Describe a basic daily routine using present tense reflexive verbs
modules.description: Tell time, discuss dates, and describe daily routines using reflexive verbs.

modules.title: Navigating Food and Dining
modules.focus: Apply food vocabulary and restaurant phrases to confidently order meals and interact with waitstaff.
objectives: Categorize food and beverage vocabulary by meal type
Use the formal 'usted' conjugation to politely address waitstaff
Order food using 'querer' and 'me gustaría'
Ask for the bill and understand basic payment vocabulary
modules.description: Apply food vocabulary and restaurant phrases to confidently order meals and interact with waitstaff.

modules.title: Asking for Directions and Getting Around
modules.focus: Identify places in the city and use prepositions of place to ask for and give basic directions.
objectives: Name common city locations and modes of transportation
Use prepositions of place (al lado de, cerca de, delante de, etc.) accurately
Ask for directions using '¿Dónde está...?' and '¿Cómo llego a...?'
Understand and give basic directional commands (gire, siga, cruce)
modules.description: Identify places in the city and use prepositions of place to ask for and give basic directions.

modules.title: Introduction to the Past Tense
modules.focus: Conjugate regular and common irregular verbs in the preterite tense to describe completed past actions.
objectives: Differentiate between the preterite and imperfect tenses for past actions
Conjugate regular -ar, -er, and -ir verbs in the preterite tense
Recognize and conjugate common irregular preterite verbs (ser, ir, tener, hacer)
Narrate a simple sequence of completed events in the past
modules.description: Conjugate regular and common irregular verbs in the preterite tense to describe completed past actions.

modules.title: Building a Sustainable Learning Routine
modules.focus: Develop strategies for vocabulary retention, immersion, and setting goals for continued learning.
objectives: Implement spaced repetition systems (SRS) for long-term vocabulary retention
Find and evaluate Spanish media (podcasts, music, shows) for beginner comprehension practice
Set SMART goals for language learning progress
Identify language exchange platforms to practice speaking with native speakers
modules.description: Develop strategies for vocabulary retention, immersion, and setting goals for continued learning.

modules.title: Spanish Pronunciation and First Words
modules.content:
Picture this: You’ve memorized the phrase for "Where is the bathroom?" (*¿Dónde está el baño?*). You walk up to a shopkeeper in Madrid, confidently recite the phrase, and the shopkeeper just stares at you blankly. You repeat it slower, but still, no recognition. Frustrated, you eventually resort to wild hand gestures, and finally, they light up and point you down the hall. 

What went wrong? Your vocabulary was perfect, but your pronunciation betrayed you. You likely pronounced the word *dónde* so it sounded like "don-day," and *baño* like "ban-yo." To an English-speaking ear, this sounds close enough. To a Spanish speaker, the vowels are so distorted that the word becomes unrecognizable.

The good news is that Spanish pronunciation is incredibly forgiving once you know the rules. Unlike English, where "read," "red," and "lead" can rhyme or not depending on entirely unrelated words, Spanish is a **phonetic language**. This means words are pronounced exactly as they are spelled. Once you master the sounds of the alphabet, you can read any Spanish word out loud perfectly—even if you have no idea what it means.

## The Foundation: Spanish Vowels

If vowels are the heart of English words, they are the entire cardiovascular system of Spanish words. English vowels are "lazy"; they shift and drift depending on the word (think of the "a" in *father*, *cake*, and *apple*). Spanish vowels are sharp, crisp, and never change their sound. 

There are only five Spanish vowels: **A, E, I, O, U**. To pronounce them correctly, you must keep your tongue and jaw relatively tense and fixed. 

### The Five Pure Vowels
*   **A (ah):** Sounds like the "a" in *father*. Open your mouth wide. 
    *   *Example:* **a**migo (ah-mee-goh)
*   **E (eh):** Sounds like the "e" in *pet*. Keep your lips slightly stretched.
    *   *Example:* **e**ntender (ehn-tehn-dehr)
*   **I (ee):** Sounds like the "ee" in *machine*. 
    *   *Example:* **sí** (see)
*   **O (oh):** Sounds like the "o" in *boat*, but without the "w" sound at the end. Your lips should form a tight circle.
    *   *Example:* **o**cho (oh-choh)
*   **U (oo):** Sounds like the "oo" in *moon*.
    *   *Example:* **u**sted (oos-tehd)

> **Pronunciation Tip:** In English, we often round off our vowels with a glide. We say "boat" as "bo-wut." In Spanish, the vowel sound stops abruptly. Practice saying "oh" without moving your lips or jaw afterward. 

## The Spanish Consonants

While many Spanish consonants look familiar, a handful behave differently than their English counterparts. Mastering these specific letters will dramatically improve your accent.

### The Unique Consonants
*   **C:** Before *e* or *i*, it sounds like the English "s" (in Latin America) or "th" in *think* (in Spain). Before *a, o,* or *u*, it sounds like the English "k."
    *   *Examples:* **c**asa (kah-sah), **c**ielo (see-eh-loh / thee-eh-loh).
*   **G:** Before *e* or *i*, it sounds like a harsh English "h" (almost like clearing your throat). Before *a, o,* or *u*, it sounds like the "g" in *go*.
    *   *Examples:* **g**ato (gah-toh), **g**ente (hehn-teh).
*   **H:** This letter is always completely silent. 
    *   *Example:* **h**ola is pronounced "oh-lah" (not "hoh-lah").
*   **J:** Sounds like a harsh, breathy "h" in English, similar to the "ch" in the Scottish word *loch* or German *Bach*.
    *   *Example:* **j**ugo (hoo-goh).
*   **LL:** Traditionally, this double-L sounds like the "y" in *yellow* (though in some regions, it sounds like the "s" in *measure*). 
    *   *Example:* **ll**amar (yah-mahr).
*   **Ñ:** This is a completely separate letter in the Spanish alphabet (pronounced *en-yeh*). It sounds like the "ny" in *canyon*.
    *   *Example:* **ñ**i**ñ**o (nee-nyoh).

### The Infamous Rolled 'R'
The Spanish **R** causes the most anxiety for English speakers, but it just requires practice. Spanish actually has two "r" sounds:
1.  **The single tap (r):** When an 'r' appears inside a word between vowels, it is a quick tap. It sounds almost exactly like the "tt" in the English word *butter* or the "dd" in *ladder*. 
    *   *Example:* pe**r**o (peh-roh) - meaning "but".
2.  **The trill (rr):** When you see a double 'rr' in the middle of a word, or a single 'r' at the very beginning of a word, you must roll or trill it. This is produced by directing air over the tip of your tongue so it vibrates rapidly against the roof of your mouth. 
    *   *Examples:* pe**rr**o (peh-rroh) - meaning "dog". **R**ojo (rroh-hoh).

If you can’t roll your 'r' right away, don't panic. Use the single tap sound for now. Native speakers will still understand you based on context, and your tongue muscles will loosen up with time.

## Syllables and Stress: The Rhythm of Spanish

English is a stress-timed language, meaning we exaggerate certain words and swallow others. Spanish is a **syllable-timed language**, meaning every syllable takes up roughly the same amount of time. It sounds much more rapid and even to English ears. 

Getting the stress right is vital. Put the emphasis on the wrong syllable, and you might accidentally call your mother (*mamá*) a breast or mammary gland (*mama*). 

Spanish has three strict rules for where the vocal stress falls:

### Rule 1: Words ending in a vowel, N, or S
If a word ends in a vowel, an *n*, or an *s*, the natural stress falls on the **second-to-last** syllable.
*   *Example:* **CA**-sa (house)
*   *Example:* ha-BLAN (they speak)
*   *Example:* LI-bros (books)

### Rule 2: Words ending in other consonants
If a word ends in any consonant other than *n* or *s*, the natural stress falls on the **last** syllable.
*   *Example:* es-PA-ñol (Spanish)
*   *Example:* re-LOJ (clock)
*   *Example:* us-TED (you, formal)

### Rule 3: Written Accents break the rules
Whenever a word breaks Rule 1 or Rule 2, it must carry a written accent mark (**á, é, í, ó, ú**) over the stressed vowel. The accent mark tells you, "Ignore the rules, emphasize me."
*   *Example:* jo-VEN (young) follows Rule 2. But **JÓ**-ven (wait, that's not right—the word is *joven*). Let's look at a real breaker: **LÁ**-piz (pencil) ends in a consonant, so it should end on the stress (la-PIZ). Because it stresses the second-to-last syllable, it gets an accent: **lá**piz.
*   *Example:* **MÚ**-si-ca ends in a vowel, so it should naturally stress the second-to-last syllable (*mu-SI-ca*). Because it stresses the third-to-last, it gets an accent: **mú**sica.

> **Jargon Check:** A **diphthong** is when two vowels blend together into a single sound (like the "ue" in *bueno*, pronounced "bweh-noh"). If a written accent mark sits over the weaker vowel in a diphthong, it forces the vowels to split into two separate syllables. For example, **día** (day) is pronounced "DEE-ah," not "dyah."

## First Words: Greetings and Farewells

Now that you know how to pronounce Spanish words flawlessly, let’s put it into practice with your first actual conversations. 

### Greetings
The time of day dictates which greeting you use. Notice how the final 's' or 'd' is often dropped in casual speech by native speakers, but as a beginner, it is best to pronounce every letter.

*   **Hola** (oh-lah): Hello. Can be used at any time, day or night.
*   **Buenos días** (bweh-nohs dee-ahs): Good morning. (Literally: "Good days")
*   **Buenas tardes** (bweh-nahs tar-dehs): Good afternoon.
*   **Buenas noches** (bweh-nahs noh-chehs): Good evening / Good night.

### Farewells
When parting ways, you have several options depending on the formality of the situation.

*   **Adiós** (ah-dyohs): Goodbye. Used when you won't see the person for a while, or don't expect to see them again that day.
*   **Hasta luego** (ahs-tah lweh-goh): See you later. Very common in everyday situations.
*   **Hasta mañana** (ahs-tah mah-nyah-nah): See you tomorrow.
*   **Chao** (chow): Bye. Borrowed from Italian, this is incredibly common in casual Latin American Spanish.

## Making Introductions

When meeting someone new, you’ll want to tell them your name and ask for theirs. 

*   **Me llamo...** (meh yah-moh): My name is... (Literally: "I call myself")
*   **¿Cómo te llamas?** (koh-moh teh yah-mahs): What is your name? (Informal, used with peers or children).
*   **¿Cómo se llama usted?** (koh-moh seh yah-mah oos-tehd): What is your name? (Formal, used with elders or in professional settings).

> **Scenario: A First Meeting**
> You walk into a coffee shop in Mexico City and strike up a conversation with the barista.
> **You:** *Hola, buenos días. Me llamo Sarah.* (Hello, good morning. My name is Sarah.)
> **Barista:** *Mucho gusto, Sarah. Yo me llamo Carlos.* (Nice to meet you, Sarah. My name is Carlos.)

In this scenario, the barista uses **Mucho gusto** (moo-choh goos-toh), which means "Nice to meet you" or literally "Much pleasure." You can also respond to an introduction with **Igualmente** (eeg-wahl-mehn-teh), meaning "Likewise."

## Polite Expressions: The Social Lubricant

Politeness goes a long way in Spanish-speaking cultures. Using these phrases will instantly mark you as a respectful traveler or conversationalist, even if your vocabulary is limited.

*   **Por favor** (pohr fah-vohr): Please. 
*   **Gracias** (grah-see-ahs): Thank you.
*   **De nada** (deh nah-dah): You're welcome. (Literally: "Of nothing")
*   **Perdón** (pehr-dohn): Excuse me / Pardon me. (Used if you bump into someone or need to get past someone).
*   **Disculpe** (dees-kool-peh): Excuse me. (Used to get someone's attention, like a waiter or a shop clerk).
*   **Lo siento** (loh see-ehn-toh): I'm sorry. (Used to express sympathy or apologize for a mistake).

> **Cultural Note:** In many Spanish-speaking countries, entering a small shop or a waiting room and greeting everyone with a general *Buenos días* is expected. Walking in silently is often considered rude. Similarly, when asking for something, dropping the *por favor* can make a request sound like a harsh command. Always tack it onto the end of your sentences!

## Key Takeaways

*   Spanish is a highly phonetic language; words are pronounced exactly as they are spelled.
*   Spanish vowels (**a, e, i, o, u**) have one pure, unchanging sound each. Avoid the lazy glides common in English vowels.
*   Unique consonants include the silent **h**, the harsh **j** and **g** (before e/i), the "ny" sound of **ñ**, and the rolled/trilled **r**.
*   Natural stress falls on the second-to-last syllable for words ending in a vowel, *n*, or *s*. It falls on the last syllable for words ending in other consonants.
*   Written accents (**á, é, í, ó, ú**) override natural stress rules and tell you exactly which syllable to emphasize.
*   Mastering basic greetings (*hola, buenos días*), farewells (*adiós, hasta luego*), and polite expressions (*por favor, gracias*) provides the foundation for basic, respectful interactions.

modules.title: Nouns, Gender, and Numbers
modules.content:
## The Building Blocks: What is a Noun?

Imagine you are walking through a bustling market in Mexico City. You see *el libro* (the book) on a table, *la manzana* (the apple) in a basket, and *los zapatos* (the shoes) in a shop window. You want to buy two apples and three books, but suddenly you realize the words for "the" and "two" seem to change depending on what you are pointing at. 

To navigate this market, you need to master the basic building blocks of the Spanish language: nouns. 

A **noun** is a word that represents a person, place, thing, or idea. In English, we have nouns like "dog," "house," or "happiness." In Spanish, nouns are written slightly differently because they carry a built-in piece of information that English nouns usually do not: **gender**. 

**Jargon Check:** In grammar, **gender** refers to a category that classifies words. In Spanish, every single noun is either **masculine** or **feminine**. This has nothing to do with biological sex or gender identity. A table (*la mesa*) is feminine, and a book (*el libro*) is masculine. The gender of a noun is simply a grammatical feature that dictates how other words around it must look. 

Because Spanish is a phonetic language, you can usually figure out a noun's gender just by looking at how it ends. 

## Masculine and Feminine Nouns

The vast majority of Spanish nouns follow a simple, predictable rule based on their final vowel. 

### The General Rule: O vs. A
If a noun ends in the letter **-o**, it is almost always **masculine**.
If a noun ends in the letter **-a**, it is almost always **feminine**.

Let's look at some examples, using the vowel pronunciations we established in Chapter 1:

**Masculine (ends in -o):**
*   **El libro** (ehl lee-broh) - the book
*   **El gato** (ehl gah-toh) - the cat (remember the single tap *r* and the soft *t* from Chapter 1)
*   **El carro** (ehl kah-rroh) - the car (using the trill *rr*)

**Feminine (ends in -a):**
*   **La casa** (lah kah-sah) - the house
*   **La manzana** (lah mahn-sah-nah) - the apple
*   **La silla** (lah see-yah) - the chair (utilizing the *LL* sound)

### Nouns Ending in Consonants or Other Vowels
What happens when a word doesn't end in -o or -a? The rules become a bit more nuanced, but there are still strong patterns.

**Nouns ending in -dad, -tad, -tud, -ción, -sión, and -umbre** are almost always **feminine**. Notice that many of these endings are similar to English words ending in "-ty" or "-tion".
*   **La ciudad** (lah seeoo-dahd) - the city
*   **La libertad** (lah lee-behr-tahd) - the liberty
*   **La canción** (lah kahn-see-ohn) - the song
*   **La televisión** (lah teh-leh-bee-see-ohn) - the television

**Nouns ending in -ma and -ta** are often of Greek origin and are usually **masculine**, even though they end in -a. This is a very common stumbling block for beginners.
*   **El problema** (ehl proh-bleh-mah) - the problem
*   **El sistema** (ehl see-steh-mah) - the system
*   **El planeta** (ehl plah-neh-tah) - the planet

**Nouns ending in other letters (like -l, -n, -r, -s, or vowels like -e)** generally require memorization, though many are masculine. 
*   **El árbol** (ehl ahr-bohl) - the tree (masculine)
*   **La clase** (lah klah-seh) - the class (feminine)

### Nouns that Defy the Rules
A few nouns change their meaning entirely based on whether they are treated as masculine or feminine. 
*   **El cometa** = the comet
*   **La cometa** = the kite

There are also a few words that end in -o but are feminine, and words that end in -a but are masculine. The most common example you will encounter early on is **la mano** (lah mah-noh), meaning "the hand." When you learn a new noun, it is always best to learn it together with its definite article (like *el* or *la*) so you naturally memorize its gender at the same time.

## Definite and Indefinite Articles

Because Spanish nouns have gender, the words that introduce them—called **articles**—must also have gender. 

**Jargon Check:** An **article** is a small word that goes before a noun to indicate whether it is specific or general. English uses "the" (definite) and "a/an" (indefinite).

In Spanish, articles act like chameleons. They change their ending to match both the gender (masculine/feminine) and the number (singular/plural) of the noun they accompany.

### Definite Articles ("The")
Definite articles point to a specific item. "Pass me *the* apple" (not just any apple, but the one we are currently looking at).

*   **El** (masculine, singular) → **El libro** (The book)
*   **La** (feminine, singular) → **La manzana** (The apple)
*   **Los** (masculine, plural) → **Los libros** (The books)
*   **Las** (feminine, plural) → **Las manzanas** (The apples)

### Indefinite Articles ("A/An/Some")
Indefinite articles point to a general, non-specific item. "Pass me *an* apple" (any apple will do).

*   **Un** (masculine, singular) → **Un libro** (A book)
*   **Una** (feminine, singular) → **Una manzana** (An apple)
*   **Unos** (masculine, plural) → **Unos libros** (Some books)
*   **Unas** (feminine, plural) → **Unas manzanas** (Some apples)

### The Gender of People and Animals
When referring to living creatures, the article and the noun ending change to reflect the biological sex of the individual.
*   **El niño** (The boy) / **La niña** (The girl)
*   **El gato** (The male cat) / **La gata** (The female cat)
*   **Un doctor** (A male doctor) / **Una doctora** (A female doctor)

## Forming Plural Nouns

Making a noun plural in Spanish is highly systematic. Because Spanish is a syllable-timed language, adding a syllable to make a word plural keeps the rhythm of the language intact. The rules depend on what letter the singular noun ends with.

### Rule 1: Nouns ending in a vowel
If a noun ends in a vowel (**A, E, I, O, U**), you simply add an **-s** to the end.
*   *La casa* → **Las casas** (The houses)
*   *El libro* → **Los libros** (The books)
*   *El taxi* → **Los taxis** (The taxis)

### Rule 2: Nouns ending in a consonant
If a noun ends in a consonant (any letter that is not a vowel), you add **-es** to the end.
*   *La ciudad* → **Las ciudades** (The cities)
*   *El animal* → **Los animales** (The animals)
*   *El reloj* → **Los relojes** (The watches/clocks) — Notice that the *j* sound in *reloj* (rreh-loh) naturally takes an *e* sound before adding the *-es* to keep pronunciation smooth.

### Rule 3: Words ending in -z
If a noun ends in the letter **-z**, the *z* changes to a *c* before adding **-es**. This maintains the soft *th* (in Spain) or *s* (in Latin America) sound of the letter *c* before an *e*.
*   *La lámpara* (ends in a, so just add -s) → **Las lámparas**
*   *La voz* (ends in z) → **Las voces** (The voices)
*   *El lápiz* (ends in z) → **Los lápices** (The pencils)

**Pronunciation Tip:** When you add an *-s* to the end of a word, you are adding a new syllable. *Libro* is two syllables (LEE-broh). *Libros* is also two syllables (LEE-brohs), with the *s* attaching to the last syllable. But *reloj* is two syllables (rreh-LOH). *Relojes* becomes three syllables (rreh-LOH-hehs). Keeping track of syllables will help you maintain the natural rhythm of Spanish.

## Counting from 0 to 100

Now that you know how to make nouns plural, you need to know how to count them. Spanish numbers from 0 to 100 follow logical patterns, but they require some memorization in the teens before settling into a predictable structure.

### 0 to 10: The Foundation
These are your building blocks. Memorize these completely before moving on.
0. **Cero** (seh-roh)
1. **Uno** (oo-noh)
2. **Dos** (dohs)
3. **Tres** (trehs)
4. **Cuatro** (kwah-troh)
5. **Cinco** (seen-koh)
6. **Seis** (sehs)
7. **Siete** (see-eh-teh)
8. **Ocho** (oh-choh)
9. **Nueve** (nweh-veh)
10. **Diez** (dee-ehs)

### 11 to 15: Unique Words
Unlike English, which has "eleven, twelve, thirteen" before settling into the "teen" pattern, Spanish has unique words for 11 through 15.
11. **Once** (ohn-seh)
12. **Doce** (doh-seh)
13. **Trece** (treh-seh)
14. **Catorce** (kah-tohr-seh)
15. **Quince** (keen-seh)

### 16 to 19: The "Ten and" Pattern
Here is where the logic begins. In Spanish, the words for 16 through 19 are literally contractions of "ten and six," "ten and seven," etc. (*Diez y seis* becomes *Dieciséis*).
16. **Dieciséis** (dee-eh-see-sehs)
17. **Diecisiete** (dee-eh-see-eh-teh)
18. **Dieciocho** (dee-eh-see-oh-choh)
19. **Diecinueve** (dee-eh-see-nweh-veh)

### 20, 30, 40, and 50: The Tens
20. **Veinte** (bayn-teh)
30. **Treinta** (trayn-tah)
40. **Cuarenta** (kwah-rehn-tah)
50. **Cincuenta** (seen-kwen-tah)

### 21 to 29: A Slight Variation
The 20s have their own slightly unique contraction pattern. Instead of *veinte y uno*, it becomes *veintiuno*.
21. **Veintiuno** (bayn-tee-oo-noh)
22. **Veintidós** (bayn-tee-dohs)
23. **Veintitrés** (bayn-tee-trehs)
*...and so on up to...*
29. **Veintinueve** (bayn-tee-nweh-veh)

### 31 to 99: The Simple Formula
From the 30s onward, the pattern is incredibly consistent. You take the tens word, add the word **y** (meaning "and"), and add the units word. 

**Formula:** [Tens] + y + [Units]
*   31: Treinta y uno (trayn-tah ee oo-noh)
*   42: Cuarenta y dos (kwah-rehn-tah ee dohs)
*   53: Cincuenta y tres (seen-kwen-tah ee trehs)
*   64: Sesenta y cuatro (seh-sehn-tah ee kwah-troh)
*   75: Setenta y cinco (seh-tehn-tah ee seen-koh)
*   86: Ochenta y seis (oh-chehn-tah ee sehs)
*   97: Noventa y siete (noh-vehn-tah ee see-eh-teh)

Here are the rest of the tens to complete your list up to 100:
60. **Sesenta** (seh-sehn-tah)
70. **Setenta** (seh-tehn-tah)
80. **Ochenta** (oh-chehn-tah)
90. **Noventa** (noh-vehn-tah)
100. **Cien** (see-ehn) 

*(Note: "Cien" is used for exactly 100. If you want to say 101, it becomes "ciento uno", but for now, focus on reaching 100).*

## Putting It All Together: A Market Scenario

Let’s return to our market scenario in Mexico City. You are standing in front of a fruit stall. The vendor asks what you would like. You want to use your new knowledge of articles, plurals, and numbers to make your purchase.

You point to the apples. You know "apple" is *la manzana*. Because you want more than one, you apply the vowel plural rule to get *las manzanas*. You want four of them. 

> "Quiero **cuatro manzanas**, por favor." 
> *(I want four apples, please.)*

Next, you point to the books on a nearby table. "Book" is *el libro*. You apply the vowel plural rule to get *los libros*. You want two of them. 

> "Y **dos libros**." 
> *(And two books.)*

Finally, you see some watches. "Watch" is *el reloj*. Because it ends in a consonant, you apply the consonant plural rule to get *los relojes*. You want one.

> "Y **un reloj**." 
> *(And a watch.)*

By understanding the gender of the noun, you knew exactly which definite article (*las, los, el*) to use. By knowing the pluralization rules, you could accurately describe the quantity of items you wanted. And by knowing your numbers, you could specify exactly how many to put in your basket.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Noun Gender:** Every Spanish noun is either masculine or feminine. Nouns ending in **-o** are usually masculine, and nouns ending in **-a** are usually feminine. 
*   **Exceptions:** Nouns ending in **-dad, -ción, -sión** are feminine. Nouns ending in **-ma** and **-ta** (often of Greek origin) are usually masculine.
*   **Definite Articles ("The"):** Use **el** (masculine singular), **la** (feminine singular), **los** (masculine plural), and **las** (feminine plural).
*   **Indefinite Articles ("A/Some"):** Use **un** (masculine singular), **una** (feminine singular), **unos** (masculine plural), and **unas** (feminine plural).
*   **Pluralization Rules:** 
    *   If a noun ends in a vowel, add **-s** (*gato → gatos*).
    *   If a noun ends in a consonant, add **-es** (*animal → animales*).
    *   If a noun ends in **-z**, change the *z* to *c* and add **-es** (*lápiz → lápices*).
*   **Numbers 0-100:** Numbers 0-15 must be memorized. 16-19 are contractions of *diez y* (ten and). From 30 onward, compound numbers are formed by combining the tens word, the word *y* (and), and the units number (e.g., *treinta y uno*).

modules.title: Subject Pronouns and Present Tense Regular Verbs
modules.content:
## The Engine of Spanish: Verbs and the Present Tense

Imagine you are sitting in a sunny plaza in Madrid. A friend points to a dog chasing a pigeon and says, "El perro **corre**." You don't need a dictionary to picture a brown blur of fur sprinting across the cobblestones. 

Why? Because **corre** is the engine of that sentence. It provides the action. 

In Chapter 1, you learned your first words (*Hola*, *Buenos días*), and in Chapter 2, you learned how to navigate the gender and number of nouns (like *asa* and *ato*). But to actually do anything in Spanish—to describe what you do, what you want, or what you need—you need verbs. 

**Jargon Check:** A **verb** is a word that expresses an action (to run, to eat) or a state of being (to be, to exist). 

Before we can make verbs run, eat, or exist, we have to know exactly *who* is performing the action. For that, we need subject pronouns.

## Who is Doing the Action? Subject Pronouns

In English, we have a small set of words we use to replace people's names so we don't sound repetitive. Instead of saying "Maria eats and Maria sleeps," we say "Maria eats and **she** sleeps." 

These replacement words are called **subject pronouns**. They tell us who is doing the action (the subject of the sentence). Spanish has subject pronouns too, but there are a few important differences from English.

### The Full List of Spanish Subject Pronouns

Let's look at the complete set of Spanish subject pronouns, grouped by perspective:

**First Person (The Speaker):**
*   **Yo** (I)
*   **Nosotros / Nosotras** (We)

**Second Person (The Listener):**
*   **Tú** (You - informal, singular)
*   **Vosotros / Vosotras** (You all - informal, plural; used primarily in Spain)
*   **Usted (Ud.)** (You - formal, singular)
*   **Ustedes (Uds.)** (You all - plural; used formally in Spain, and for *both* formal and informal in Latin America)

**Third Person (Others):**
*   **Él** (He)
*   **Ella** (She)
*   **Ellos / Ellas** (They)

### The Gender Split

As you learned in the previous chapter, Spanish nouns have gender. When a pronoun represents a mixed group of males and females, the masculine form is used by default. 

*   If you and your male friend are going to the store, you say **nosotros**. 
*   If you and your three female friends are going to the store, you say **nosotras**.
*   The same applies to **ellos** (a mixed group of "them") and **ellas** (a strictly female group of "them").

### The "You" Distinction: Formal vs. Informal

English makes it easy on us: "you" is "you," whether you are talking to a baby, your best friend, your boss, or a crowd of a thousand people. Spanish, like many languages, draws a line between people you are close with (informal) and people you want to show respect to (formal).

*   **Tú:** Use this with friends, family, peers your own age, children, and pets. It implies familiarity and closeness.
*   **Usted (Ud.):** Use this with strangers, elders, people in positions of authority (like a doctor or a boss), or anyone you want to show respect to. 
*   **Ustedes (Uds.):** This is the plural of "you." If you are in Latin America, you will use *ustedes* for *any* group of people, whether you are close with them or not. If you are in Spain, you use *vosotros/vosotras* for informal groups, and *ustedes* strictly for formal groups. 

**Pronunciation Tip:** *Usted* and *Ustedes* are often abbreviated in writing as *Ud.* and *Uds.* When speaking, however, you always say the full word. Remember from Chapter 1 that Spanish is a phonetic language, so pronounce every syllable: *oos-TEHD* and *oos-TEH-dehs*.

## The Three Verb Families: -AR, -ER, and -IR

Now that we know *who* is doing the action, let's look at the verbs themselves. 

When you look up a verb in a Spanish dictionary, you will find it in its raw, unconjugated form. This is called the **infinitive**. 

**Jargon Check:** The **infinitive** is the base form of a verb. In English, it includes the word "to" (to speak, to eat, to live). In Spanish, infinitives are a single word that always ends in either **-ar**, **-er**, or **-ir**.

These three endings are incredibly important because they sort every regular Spanish verb into one of three families. Once you know how a family behaves, you can make any verb in that family work for you. 

Here are examples of the three families:

1.  **-AR verbs:** *hablar* (to speak), *cantar* (to sing), *trabajar* (to work)
2.  **-ER verbs:** *comer* (to eat), *beber* (to drink), *leer* (to read)
3.  **-IR verbs:** *vivir* (to live), *escribir* (to write), *abrir* (to open)

## The Mechanics of Conjugation

If you want to say "I speak" in Spanish, you cannot just say "Yo hablar." That literally translates to "I to speak." To make the verb match the subject, you have to **conjugate** it.

**Jargon Check:** **Conjugation** is the process of changing a verb's ending to match the subject (who is doing the action) and the tense (when it is happening).

Conjugating a regular verb in the present tense is a simple two-step process:

1.  **Drop the infinitive ending.** Remove the *-ar*, *-er*, or *-ir* from the end of the verb. What remains is called the **stem**. 
    *   *Hablar* minus *-ar* leaves the stem **habl-**.
2.  **Add the correct present-tense ending.** You attach a new ending to the stem based on who is doing the action.

Let's break down the endings for each family.

### Conjugating Regular -AR Verbs

*-AR* verbs are the most common type of verb in Spanish. Let's use **hablar** (to speak) as our model.

To conjugate *hablar*, drop the *-ar* to get the stem *habl-*, and add these endings:

*   **Yo** habl + **o** = hablo (I speak)
*   **Tú** habl + **as** = hablas (You speak)
*   **Él / Ella / Ud.** habl + **a** = habla (He/She/You formal speak)
*   **Nosotros / Nosotras** habl + **amos** = hablamos (We speak)
*   **Vosotros / Vosotras** habl + **áis** = habláis (You all speak - Spain)
*   **Ellos / Ellas / Uds.** habl + **an** = hablan (They/You all speak)

Notice how the *-ar* ending dictates the specific set of letters you attach to the stem. 

**Pronunciation Tip:** Pay attention to the stress in these conjugations. Because Spanish is a syllable-timed language, getting the stress right is crucial for being understood. In *hablamos*, the natural stress falls on the second-to-last syllable (ha-**bla**-mos). In *habláis*, the stress falls on the last syllable because of the accent mark (ha-blá-**is**), just as we covered with the rules of written accents in Chapter 1.

### Conjugating Regular -ER Verbs

*-ER* verbs use a slightly different set of endings. Let's use **comer** (to eat). Drop the *-er* to get the stem *com-*, and add these endings:

*   **Yo** com + **o** = como (I eat)
*   **Tú** com + **es** = comes (You eat)
*   **Él / Ella / Ud.** com + **e** = come (He/She/You formal eat)
*   **Nosotros / Nosotras** com + **emos** = comemos (We eat)
*   **Vosotros / Vosotras** com + **éis** = coméis (You all eat - Spain)
*   **Ellos / Ellas / Uds.** com + **en** = comen (They/You all eat)

### Conjugating Regular -IR Verbs

*-IR* verbs are the final family. They are unique because they share the exact same endings as *-er* verbs in four of the six pronoun slots, but they have their own distinct endings for *nosotros* and *vosotros*. 

Let's use **vivir** (to live). Drop the *-ir* to get the stem *viv-*, and add these endings:

*   **Yo** viv + **o** = vivo (I live)
*   **Tú** viv + **es** = vives (You live)
*   **Él / Ella / Ud.** viv + **e** = vive (He/She/You formal live)
*   **Nosotros / Nosotras** viv + **imos** = vivimos (We live)
*   **Vosotros / Vosotras** viv + **ís** = vivís (You all live - Spain)
*   **Ellos / Ellas / Uds.** viv + **en** = viven (They/You all live)

Take a close look at *vives* and *vive*. They sound exactly the same as the *-er* endings (*comes*, *come*). The only place your ear will catch a difference between an *-er* and an *-ir* verb in the present tense is in the "we" (*nosotros*) and "you all" (*vosotros*) forms. 

## The Secret of Spanish Subjects: When to Drop the Pronoun

You have just learned how to conjugate verbs. Now you can build a sentence like: 
**Yo como una manzana.** (I eat an apple.)

This is completely correct. However, native Spanish speakers rarely speak this way. They would simply say:
**Como una manzana.**

Why? Because the verb ending does the heavy lifting. 

In English, if I just say "Eat an apple," you don't know if I mean "I eat," "You eat," or "We eat." The pronoun is mandatory. But in Spanish, the ending *-o* can only mean "I". Therefore, saying *Yo como* is redundant, like saying "I, I eat an apple." 

**The Rule:** In Spanish, you generally drop the subject pronoun because the conjugated verb already tells you who the subject is. You only include the pronoun for emphasis or to clear up ambiguity. 

For example, the *él/ella/usted* form and the *ellos/ellas/ustedes* form can sometimes be ambiguous if you don't have context. If someone walks into a room and says "Habla español," they could mean "He speaks Spanish," "She speaks Spanish," or "You (formal) speak Spanish." In those cases, you include the pronoun to clarify: *Él habla español.*

## Putting It Together: Building Simple Sentences

Let's look at a concrete scenario to see how these pieces fit together to state basic facts and actions.

**Scenario:** You are introducing a new friend, Marco, to your host family in Latin America. You want to tell them a few basic facts about him.

You know the following verbs:
*   *Trabajar* (to work)
*   *Comer* (to eat)
*   *Vivir* (to live)

You want to say: "Marco works in a hospital. He eats pizza. He lives in the city."

Let's conjugate based on the subject (Marco/He):

1.  **Marco works in a hospital.**
    *   Verb: *trabajar* (an -AR verb)
    *   Subject: He (*él*) -> ending is *-a*
    *   Conjugated verb: *trabaja*
    *   Sentence: **Marco trabaja en un hospital.** (Notice we don't need to say *Él* because Marco's name already tells us who we are talking about).

2.  **He eats pizza.**
    *   Verb: *comer* (an -ER verb)
    *   Subject: He (*él*) -> ending is *-e*
    *   Conjugated verb: *come*
    *   Sentence: **Come pizza.** (Pronoun dropped because the ending *-e* tells us it means "he eats").

3.  **He lives in the city.**
    *   Verb: *vivir* (an -IR verb)
    *   Subject: He (*él*) -> ending is *-e*
    *   Conjugated verb: *vive*
    *   Sentence: **Vive en la ciudad.**

Put it all together, and you have a fluid, natural Spanish paragraph:
*Marco trabaja en un hospital. Come pizza. Vive en la ciudad.*

### A Quick Note on Word Order

Notice the word order in the sentence *Come pizza*. In English, the standard order is Subject-Verb-Object (He eats pizza). In Spanish, because the subject is baked right into the verb (*come* = he/she eats), the default sentence structure is often just Verb-Object. 

You can say *Él come pizza* if you want to emphasize that *he* (and not someone else) eats pizza, but *Come pizza* is the most natural, standard way to state a simple fact.

## Practice: Categorizing and Conjugating

Before we wrap up, let's test your understanding with a quick mental exercise. Read the following infinitive verbs, identify their family, and think about how you would conjugate them for "yo" (I).

1.  **Beber** (to drink)
    *   Ending: *-er*
    *   Stem: *beb-*
    *   Yo form: *bebo* (I drink)

2.  **Escribir** (to write)
    *   Ending: *-ir*
    *   Stem: *escrib-*
    *   Yo form: *escribo* (I write)

3.  **Cantar** (to sing)
    *   Ending: *-ar*
    *   Stem: *cant-*
    *   Yo form: *canto* (I sing)

If you can identify the ending, find the stem, and recall the correct set of letters to attach, you have unlocked the ability to use thousands of regular Spanish verbs in the present tense. 

## Key Takeaways

*   **Subject pronouns** replace names and tell you who is doing the action. Spanish distinguishes between informal (**tú**) and formal (**usted**) "you," as well as plural "you all" (**ustedes** / **vosotros**).
*   **Infinitives** are the base form of verbs. In Spanish, they always end in **-ar**, **-er**, or **-ir**.
*   **Conjugation** is the process of dropping the infinitive ending to find the **stem**, and adding a new ending that matches the subject pronoun.
*   **-AR verbs** (like *hablar*) use the endings: *-o, -as, -a, -amos, -áis, -an*.
*   **-ER verbs** (like *comer*) use the endings: *-o, -es, -e, -emos, -éis, -en*.
*   **-IR verbs** (like *vivir*) use the endings: *-o, -es, -e, -imos, -ís, -en*.
*   Because the verb ending clearly indicates the subject, Spanish speakers frequently **drop the subject pronoun** (saying *Como* instead of *Yo como*) unless they need it for emphasis or clarity.

modules.title: Forming Sentences: Questions and Negation
modules.content:
## Building Basic Sentences

Imagine you are walking through a market in Mexico City. You see a stall selling beautiful handmade leather bags. You want to tell the vendor that you want a bag, and then you need to ask how much it costs. To survive and thrive in a Spanish-speaking environment, you need to do two things: make statements and ask questions. 

In previous chapters, you learned how to identify **nouns** and their **gender** (masculine and feminine), and how to pair **subject pronouns** (like *yo*, *tú*, *nosotros*) with **present tense regular verbs** (like *hablo*, *comes*, *vive*). Now, it is time to string those pieces together. 

A **declarative sentence** is simply a sentence that makes a statement, provides a fact, or expresses an opinion. In English, the standard word order is Subject-Verb-Object (SVO). For example: "I (subject) eat (verb) the apple (object)."

Spanish uses the exact same standard word order. 

**Subject + Verb + Object**

Let’s look at this using vocabulary you already know:
*   **Yo** (Subject) **hablo** (Verb) **español** (Object). -> *I speak Spanish.*
*   **Tú** (Subject) **comes** (Verb) **pan** (Object). -> *You eat bread.*
*   **Nosotros** (Subject) **bebemos** (Verb) **agua** (Object). -> *We drink water.*

Because Spanish is a "pro-drop" language—meaning the subject pronoun is often dropped because the verb ending already tells you who is doing the action—many declarative sentences simply begin with the verb. 

*   **Hablo** español. -> *I speak Spanish.* (The *-o* ending tells us it means "I").
*   **Comes** pan. -> *You eat bread.* (The *-es* ending tells us it means "You").
*   **Beben** agua. -> *They drink water.* (The *-en* ending tells us it means "They").

### Adjectives and Word Order
When you want to describe an object, you use an **adjective** (a word that describes a noun). In English, we put the adjective before the noun: "the red apple." In Spanish, the adjective goes *after* the noun it describes: "the apple red."

*   El **gato negro**. -> *The black cat.* (Literally: The cat black).
*   La **casa grande**. -> *The big house.* (Literally: The house big).

**Jargon Check:** An **adjective** is a word that modifies or describes a noun. It gives you more information about an object, person, or place, such as its color, size, or shape.

## Asking Yes/No Questions

Now, let's go back to that market in Mexico City. You see the vendor make a leather bag. You want to ask, "Do you make the bags?" 

In English, asking a yes/no question usually requires adding a "helper" verb like *do*, *does*, *is*, or *are* to the beginning of the sentence. You change "You eat bread" to "Do you eat bread?"

Spanish is much simpler. To turn almost any declarative statement into a yes/no question, you do not need to rearrange the words or add any new verbs. You only need to do two things:

1.  Use **inverted question marks**.
2.  Change your **intonation** (the rise and fall of your voice).

### Inverted Punctuation

Because Spanish is a **phonetic language**—meaning words are spelled exactly as they sound—written Spanish goes out of its way to help you read it aloud. When you ask a question in Spanish, you must place an upside-down question mark (¿) at the very beginning of the question, and a regular question mark (?) at the end.

*   **¿**Tú comes pan**?** -> *Do you eat bread?*
*   **¿**Hablas español**?** -> *Do you speak Spanish?*

**Pronunciation Tip:** The upside-down question mark is your visual cue. When you reach a ¿ while reading, your brain knows immediately that the sentence is a question, allowing you to apply the correct questioning tone from the very first syllable rather than waiting until the end of the sentence.

### Rising Intonation

To make it sound like a question when you speak, you simply raise the pitch of your voice at the end of the sentence, just as you do in English. 

Statement (voice falls at the end): Hablas español. (*You speak Spanish.*)
Question (voice rises at the end): ¿Hablas español? (*Do you speak Spanish?*)

If you are speaking, the words are exactly the same. The only difference is the melody of your voice. 

## Using Interrogative Words

Yes/no questions are highly useful, but they don't get you very far if you need specific information. If you want to know *where* the bathroom is, *when* the train leaves, or *what* that delicious food is, you need **interrogative words**.

**Jargon Check:** An **interrogative word** is a question word used to ask for specific information. In English, these are words like *who, what, where, when, why,* and *how*.

In Spanish, all interrogative words contain an accent mark. As we covered in our pronunciation chapter, an accent mark (like the *í* in *día*) tells you exactly which syllable to stress. When you write these question words, they must carry their accent marks. When you speak them, you emphasize the stressed syllable.

Here are the most common interrogative words for beginners:

### Qué (What)
**Qué** is used to ask "what" or "which." 
*   **¿Qué es esto?** -> *What is this?*
*   **¿Qué comes?** -> *What do you eat?*

### Dónde (Where)
**Dónde** is used to ask about location. 
*   **¿Dónde está el baño?** -> *Where is the bathroom?*
*   **¿Dónde vives?** -> *Where do you live?*

### Cuándo (When)
**Cuándo** is used to ask about time or dates.
*   **¿Cuándo comes?** -> *When do you eat?*
*   **¿Cuándo trabajas?** -> *When do you work?*

### Cómo (How)
**Cómo** is used to ask about manner, condition, or state. You also use it to ask "What is [someone] like?"
*   **¿Cómo estás?** -> *How are you?*
*   **¿Cómo hablas?** -> *How do you speak?* (e.g., loudly, softly, quickly)

### Por qué (Why)
**Por qué** (written as two separate words) is used to ask for a reason. 
*   **¿Por qué comes pan?** -> *Why do you eat bread?*
*   **¿Por qué trabajas?** -> *Why do you work?*

*Note:* The answer to a *por qué* question uses **porque** (written as one word, no accent mark), which means "because." 
*   ¿**Por qué** comes pan? Porque es delicioso. (*Why do you eat bread? Because it is delicious.*)

### Quién / Quiénes (Who)
**Quién** is used to ask "who" when referring to a single person. 
*   **¿Quién habla?** -> *Who is speaking?*

If you are asking about more than one person, you use **quiénes** to match the plural subject.
*   **¿Quiénes hablan?** -> *Who are speaking?* / *Who (plural) is speaking?*

### Word Order with Interrogative Words

When you ask a question using an interrogative word, the word order shifts slightly. The interrogative word always goes first, followed by the verb, and then the subject (if you include it). 

**Interrogative Word + Verb + Subject**

*   **¿Qué** (Interrogative) **come** (Verb) **María** (Subject)? -> *What does María eat?*
*   **¿Dónde** (Interrogative) **viven** (Verb) **ellos** (Subject)? -> *Where do they live?*

Most of the time, you will drop the subject pronoun entirely, as the verb ending gives it away. "¿Dónde viven?" is perfectly natural and complete on its own.

## Making Sentences Negative

You won't always want to say yes or agree to things. Sometimes you need to say, "I don't eat meat," or "I don't speak French." 

Making a sentence negative in Spanish is remarkably straightforward. In English, we use a variety of negative words like "don't", "doesn't", "isn't", "aren't", or "am not", and we place them both before and after the verb (e.g., "I **do not** eat **meat**"). 

In Spanish, you only need one word: **no**. 

To make any sentence negative, place the word **no** directly before the conjugated verb. 

**Subject + no + Verb + Object**

Let's look at our earlier declarative sentences and make them negative:
*   Yo **no** hablo español. -> *I do not speak Spanish.*
*   Tú **no** comes pan. -> *You do not eat bread.*
*   Nosotros **no** bebemos agua. -> *We do not drink water.*

Just like with declarative sentences, if you drop the subject pronoun, the *no* simply goes right before the verb:
*   **No** hablo español.
*   **No** comes pan.
*   **No** bebemos agua.

### Answering Questions Negatively

When someone asks you a yes/no question, you can use this same negative structure to answer them. 

**Scenario:** 
A friend asks you: **¿Bebes café?** (*Do you drink coffee?*)
You reply: **No, no bebo café.** (*No, I do not drink coffee.*)

Notice that you use the word "no" twice in this reply. 
1. The first **no** is simply answering the question ("No").
2. The second **no** is making the verb negative ("I do not drink"). 

This is entirely normal in Spanish and sounds perfectly natural. 

### Negative Questions

You can also ask a question in the negative. You simply combine the inverted question marks, the interrogative word (if you are using one), and the negative *no*. 

*   **¿No comes carne?** -> *Don't you eat meat?*
*   **¿Por qué no hablas?** -> *Why aren't you speaking?*

## Putting It All Together

Let’s look at a realistic scenario to see how statements, questions, and negation work together in a single conversation.

**Scenario:** You are at a café in Madrid. 

You want to tell the waiter what you want (Declarative):
*   **Yo bebo agua.** (*I drink water.*)
*   **Como pan.** (*I eat bread.*)

The waiter wants to confirm your order (Yes/No Question):
*   **¿Bebes agua?** (*Do you drink water?*)
*   **¿Comes pan?** (*Do you eat bread?*)

You want to know what kind of bread it is (Interrogative Question):
*   **¿Qué es esto?** (*What is this?*)

The waiter tells you, but you don't want butter (Negation):
*   **No, no como mantequilla.** (*No, I do not eat butter.*)

You need to know where the restroom is (Interrogative Question):
*   **¿Dónde está el baño?** (*Where is the bathroom?*)

With just a handful of verbs, some basic nouns, and the grammar rules from this chapter, you can successfully navigate a basic interaction. You can state your needs, ask for vital information, and decline things you don't want.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Standard Word Order:** Spanish uses a Subject + Verb + Object structure, just like English. However, the subject pronoun is frequently dropped because the verb ending already indicates who is doing the action.
*   **Adjective Placement:** Adjectives generally come *after* the noun they describe (e.g., *el gato negro*).
*   **Yes/No Questions:** To ask a question that requires a yes or no answer, simply use inverted question marks (¿?) at the beginning and end of the sentence and raise the pitch of your voice at the end. No extra "helper" verbs are needed.
*   **Interrogative Words:** To ask for specific information, use question words at the beginning of the sentence. The most common are **qué** (what), **dónde** (where), **cuándo** (when), **cómo** (how), **por qué** (why), and **quién/quienes** (who). Always include their accent marks in writing.
*   **Negation:** To make any sentence negative, place the word **no** directly before the conjugated verb. To answer a question negatively, use *no* twice: once to answer, and once to negate the verb (e.g., *No, no bebo café*).

modules.title: The Two 'To Be' Verbs: Ser and Estar
modules.content:
## The Challenge of Two "To Be" Verbs

Imagine you are at a café in Madrid with a new Spanish-speaking friend. You want to tell them two simple things: "I am tall" and "I am happy." 

In English, this is effortless. You just use the verb "to be" for both statements. But in Spanish, you have to make a choice. You would say *"Soy alto"* (I am tall) and *"Estoy feliz"* (I am happy). 

If you accidentally swap them, and say *"Estoy alto"* (I am temporarily tall) or *"Soy feliz"* (I am a happy person by nature), your friend will still understand you, but the meaning shifts in a strange way. 

Spanish splits the English verb "to be" into two entirely different verbs: **ser** and **estar**. The general rule of thumb is that **ser** is used for permanent traits, while **estar** is used for temporary states. However, to truly master these verbs, we need to look at the specific situations where each one applies. 

Before we learn how to use them, we first need to learn how to conjugate them.

## Conjugating Ser and Estar in the Present Tense

In Chapter 3, we learned that conjugating a regular verb means changing its ending to match the subject pronoun (I, you, he, she, etc.). However, **ser** and **estar** are irregular verbs. This means they do not follow the standard patterns you learned for regular verbs. You simply have to memorize their forms.

**Jargon Check:** An **irregular verb** is a verb that does not follow the standard conjugation rules for its verb type. Instead of just changing the ending (like changing -ar to -o), the entire base of the word might change.

Here are the present tense conjugations for both verbs:

### Ser (To be - permanent traits)

*   **Yo** soy (I am)
*   **Tú** eres (You are)
*   **Él / Ella / Usted** es (He / She / You [formal] are)
*   **Nosotros / Nosotras** somos (We are)
*   **Vosotros / Vosotras** sois (You all are)
*   **Ellos / Ellas / Ustedes** son (They / You all [formal] are)

### Estar (To be - temporary states)

*   **Yo** estoy (I am)
*   **Tú** estás (You are)
*   **Él / Ella / Usted** está (He / She / You [formal] are)
*   **Nosotros / Nosotras** estamos (We are)
*   **Vosotros / Vosotras** estáis (You all are)
*   **Ellos / Ellas / Ustedes** están (They / You all [formal] are)

**Pronunciation Tip:** Remember from Chapter 1 that Spanish is a syllable-timed language. For the verb *estar*, pay special attention to the accents on *estás*, *está*, and *están*. The accent mark (´) tells you to put vocal emphasis on that specific syllable. Without the accent, *esta* means "this" (feminine), not "is". 

Also, remember the Spanish "r" from Chapter 1. In *ser*, you use a single tap of the tongue against the roof of your mouth. 

## When to Use Ser: The DOCTOR Acronym

Because **ser** represents the "to be" of permanent traits, identities, and inherent characteristics, it covers a wide range of essential descriptions. To help you remember when to use **ser**, you can use the acronym **DOCTOR**.

### D - Description
Use **ser** to describe the physical or basic identifying traits of a person, place, or thing. 
*   *Yo soy alto.* (I am tall.)
*   *El gato es negro.* (The cat is black.)
*   *La casa es grande.* (The house is big.)

### O - Occupation
Use **ser** to talk about what someone does for a living or their main activity. Notice that in Spanish, you do not use an article (like "un" or "a") before the occupation.
*   *Yo soy profesor.* (I am a teacher.)
*   *Ella es doctora.* (She is a doctor.)
*   *Ellos son estudiantes.* (They are students.)

### C - Characteristic
Use **ser** for personality traits and inherent qualities that define who someone or something is.
*   *Mi amigo es muy amable.* (My friend is very kind.)
*   *Los perros son leales.* (Dogs are loyal.)
*   *El libro es aburrido.* (The book is boring.)

### T - Time
Use **ser** to tell time, state the date, or talk about the day of the week. 
*   *Son las tres.* (It is three o'clock.)
*   *Hoy es lunes.* (Today is Monday.)
*   *Es el cinco de mayo.* (It is the fifth of May.)

### O - Origin
Use **ser** to say where someone or something is from. This is a permanent fact of birth or creation.
*   *Yo soy de Estados Unidos.* (I am from the United States.)
*   *El vino es de España.* (The wine is from Spain.)
*   *¿De dónde eres tú?* (Where are you from?)

### R - Relationship
Use **ser** to state family ties, friendships, and romantic relationships. 
*   *Ella es mi hermana.* (She is my sister.)
*   *Carlos es mi mejor amigo.* (Carlos is my best friend.)
*   *Nosotros somos primos.* (We are cousins.)

## When to Use Estar: The PLACE Acronym

If **ser** is the verb of "what something is," **estar** is the verb of "how or where something is right now." It handles temporary states, locations, and conditions. To remember when to use **estar**, use the acronym **PLACE**.

### P - Position
Use **estar** to describe the physical posture or position of a person or object.
*   *El niño está sentado.* (The boy is seated.)
*   *Yo estoy de pie.* (I am standing.)
*   *Las llaves están encima de la mesa.* (The keys are on top of the table.)

### L - Location
Use **estar** to say where an event, place, or object is physically located. 
*   *El baño está al lado.* (The bathroom is next door.)
*   *Madrid está en España.* (Madrid is in Spain.)
*   *¿Dónde está el libro?* (Where is the book?)

**Jargon Check:** You might wonder why location is temporary, since Madrid will never leave Spain. The trick is that **location** (where something is sitting right now) is treated as a temporary state in Spanish, even if the location rarely changes. The physical existence of Madrid is permanent (*Madrid es una ciudad*), but its geographic location uses *estar* (*Madrid está en España*).

### A - Action
Use **estar** to describe an action that is happening right now. This is known as the present progressive tense. While we will cover the grammar of this tense in detail later, you should know the basic formula: **estar** + a verb ending in *-ando* or *-iendo*.
*   *Yo estoy comiendo.* (I am eating.)
*   *Ella está leyendo.* (She is reading.)
*   *Estamos hablando español.* (We are speaking Spanish.)

### C - Condition
Use **estar** to describe the physical or mental condition of a person or object—usually something that has changed from its normal state.
*   *La sopa está fría.* (The soup is cold. — *Even if the soup is inherently a hot food, its current condition is cold.*)
*   *La puerta está abierta.* (The door is open.)
*   *Yo estoy enfermo.* (I am sick.)

### E - Emotion
Use **estar** to express how someone is feeling at a given moment. 
*   *Yo estoy feliz.* (I am happy right now.)
*   *Tú estás cansado.* (You are tired.)
*   *Ellos están enojados.* (They are angry.)

## The Adjective Trap: How the Same Word Changes Meaning

One of the most fascinating aspects of having two "to be" verbs is that you can take the exact same adjective (a describing word) and change its meaning entirely just by swapping *ser* and *estar*. 

Because *ser* defines inherent nature and *estar* defines a current state, the context changes the adjective. Here are the most common examples:

*   **Aburrido (Boring vs. Bored)**
    *   *La película es aburrida.* (The movie is boring. — *It is an inherently boring movie.*)
    *   *Yo estoy aburrido.* (I am bored. — *I am currently feeling bored.*)

*   **Listo (Smart vs. Ready)**
    *   *El niño es muy listo.* (The boy is very smart. — *He is an intelligent kid.*)
    *   *La comida está lista.* (The food is ready. — *The food is in a state of readiness to be eaten.*)

*   **Bueno (Good-natured vs. Tasty/Good-quality)**
    *   *Juan es bueno.* (Juan is a good person. — *He has a good moral character.*)
    *   *La paella está buena.* (The paella is good. — *It tastes good right now.*)

## Forming Sentences: A Practical Scenario

Let’s put this all together. You are at a networking event in Mexico City. You need to introduce yourself, describe your friend, explain how you both are feeling, and tell someone where the event is. 

Using your knowledge from Chapter 2 (Nouns, Gender, and Numbers) and Chapter 4 (Forming Sentences: Questions and Negation), combined with your new knowledge of *ser* and *estar*, you can build the following conversation:

**Stranger:** *Hola, buenos días. ¿De dónde eres?* 
(Hi, good morning. Where are you from?)
**You:** *Yo soy de Canadá. Soy programador.* 
(I am from Canada. I am a programmer.)
**Stranger:** *¿Y cómo estás hoy?* 
(And how are you today?)
**You:** *Estoy un poco cansado, pero estoy contento. Y tú, ¿cómo estás?* 
(I am a little tired, but I am happy. And you, how are you?)
**Stranger:** *Estoy bien, gracias. ¿Quién es ella?* 
(I am well, thanks. Who is she?)
**You:** *Ella es mi colega. Ella es de Argentina. Ella es muy inteligente y es arquitecta. Ahora ella está bebiendo café.* 
(She is my colleague. She is from Argentina. She is very intelligent and is an architect. Right now she is drinking coffee.)
**Stranger:** *¿Dónde está el evento?* 
(Where is the event?)
**You:** *El evento está en la sala principal.* 
(The event is in the main room.)

In this short conversation, you successfully used **ser** for origin (*soy de Canadá*), occupation (*soy programador*), relationship (*es mi colega*), and characteristic (*es muy inteligente*). You also successfully used **estar** for emotion (*estoy cansado*), action (*está bebiendo*), and location (*está en la sala principal*).

## Key Takeaways

*   Spanish uses two different verbs to express the English "to be": **ser** and **estar**.
*   **Ser** is generally used for permanent traits, identities, and inherent characteristics. Use the acronym **DOCTOR** (Description, Occupation, Characteristic, Time, Origin, Relationship) to remember when to use it.
*   **Estar** is generally used for temporary states, locations, and current conditions. Use the acronym **PLACE** (Position, Location, Action, Condition, Emotion) to remember when to use it.
*   Both verbs are highly irregular in the present tense and must be memorized. 
*   Using *ser* or *estar* with the same adjective can change its meaning entirely (e.g., *es aburrido* means "is boring," while *está aburrido* means "is bored").
*   When forming basic sentences, always ensure the verb matches the subject pronoun, just as you did with regular verbs in Chapter 3.

modules.title: Expressing Possession and Description
modules.content:
Imagine you are at a bustling café in Madrid. You set your phone on the table, and a friend accidentally picks it up, thinking it belongs to someone else. You need to quickly say, "That is *my* phone!" Or perhaps you are looking at a photo of a friend's family and want to ask, "Is she *your* sister?" 

In English, we express possession by adding words like "my," "your," "his," "her," "our," and "their" before an object. We also use apostrophe "s" (as in "Maria's book"). Spanish handles this differently. Instead of using an apostrophe to show ownership, Spanish relies on a specific set of words called **possessive adjectives**.

**Jargon Check: Adjective** 
An adjective is simply a word that describes a noun. A **possessive adjective** is a word that describes a noun by showing who owns it. In the phrase "my book," "my" is the possessive adjective describing the book.

In Spanish, possessive adjectives are placed directly *before* the noun they modify, exactly like in English. Let's look at the short-form possessive adjectives.

### The Short-Form Possessive Adjectives

Here is your complete list of short-form possessive adjectives:

*   **mi** / **mis** (my)
*   **tu** / **tus** (your - singular, informal)
*   **su** / **sus** (your - singular, formal; his; her; its)
*   **nuestro** / **nuestra** / **nuestros** / **nuestras** (our)
*   **vuestro** / **vuestra** / **vuestros** / **vuestras** (your - plural, informal - used primarily in Spain)
*   **su** / **sus** (your - plural, formal; their)

Notice that some of these words have two forms (like *mi* and *mis*), while *nuestro* and *vuestro* have four. Why? Because of gender and number. 

As we learned in "Nouns, Gender, and Numbers," Spanish nouns are either masculine or feminine, and they can be singular or plural. The rule for possessive adjectives is straightforward:
1. **They must match the *number* (singular or plural) of the object being owned, not the owner.**
2. **Only *nuestro* and *vuestro* change to match the *gender* (masculine or feminine) of the object.** The rest stay the same regardless of gender.

Let’s see this in action. If you want to say "my book" (*libro* is masculine singular), you use *mi*. If you want to say "my books," you use *mis*.

*   **mi** libro (my book)
*   **mis** libros (my books)
*   **mi** pluma (my pen - *pluma* is feminine singular)
*   **mis** plumas (my pens)

Because *nuestro* (our) and *vuestro* (your - plural) end in an "o," they act like standard Spanish nouns and adjectives. They must match both the gender and the number of the object.

*   **nuestro** perro (our dog - masculine singular)
*   **nuestra** casa (our house - feminine singular)
*   **nuestros** perros (our dogs - masculine plural)
*   **nuestras** casas (our houses - feminine plural)

### The "Su" Problem: Context is King

You might have noticed that **su** and **sus** are doing a lot of heavy lifting. They can mean "your" (formal), "his," "her," "its," "your" (plural), and "their." How do you know which one someone means?

In Spanish, context is king. If you are looking at a photo of your friend Carlos, and someone points to a woman in the photo and says, "*su* hermana," they mean "his sister." If you are speaking respectfully to an older gentleman, Mr. Gómez, and you ask about his car, "*su* coche" means "your car."

If the context is ever truly confusing, Spanish has a backup plan. You can clarify *su* by adding a prepositional phrase after the noun:
*   El coche **de él** (his car - literally "the car of him")
*   El coche **de ella** (her car)
*   El coche **de usted** (your car - formal)
*   El coche **de ellos** (their car - masculine/mixed group)
*   El coche **de ellas** (their car - feminine group)

But for everyday conversation, *su* and *sus* work perfectly fine on their own. 

## Describing the World: Adjectives in Action

Now that we can say who owns an object, let's talk about how to describe it. We established earlier that an adjective describes a noun. In English, we usually put the adjective *before* the noun: "the **red** car," "the **tall** man," "the **happy** dog."

In Spanish, the standard rule is the exact opposite: **the descriptive adjective goes *after* the noun.**

*   el coche **rojo** (the red car)
*   el hombre **alto** (the tall man)
*   el perro **feliz** (the happy dog)

### The Golden Rule of Adjectives: Agreement

In "Nouns, Gender, and Numbers," we learned that Spanish is a highly structured language where things must match. This brings us to the golden rule of Spanish adjectives: **Adjectives must agree in gender and number with the noun they modify.**

If you have a masculine singular noun, your adjective must be masculine singular. If you have a feminine plural noun, your adjective must be feminine plural.

How adjectives change depends on how they end. Let's break this down into three categories.

#### 1. Adjectives ending in "-o" 
These are the most straightforward. They have four forms.
*   **Masculine Singular:** -o (e.g., **blanco** - white)
*   **Feminine Singular:** -a (e.g., **blanca**)
*   **Masculine Plural:** -os (e.g., **blancos**)
*   **Feminine Plural:** -as (e.g., **blancas**)

*Example:* 
*   el gato blanco (the white cat)
*   la mesa blanca (the white table)
*   los gatos blancos (the white cats)
*   las mesas blancas (the white tables)

#### 2. Adjectives ending in "-e" or a consonant
Adjectives that end in "-e" (like *grande* - big, or *inteligente* - smart) or a consonant (like *azul* - blue, or *popular* - popular) do not change for gender. They only change for number. They have just two forms: singular and plural. To make them plural, you simply add an "-s" or "-es" (following the same rules you learned for making nouns plural in Chapter 2).

*   **Singular:** stays the same (e.g., **grande**, **azul**)
*   **Plural:** add -s if it ends in a vowel, add -es if it ends in a consonant (e.g., **grandes**, **azules**)

*Example:*
*   el coche grande (the big car)
*   la casa grande (the big house)
*   los coches grandes (the big cars)
*   las casas grandes (the big houses)

#### 3. Adjectives of nationality or origin
Adjectives that describe where something is from (like *español* - Spanish, or *alemán* - German) follow a special rule. Even if they end in a consonant, they still have four forms to match both gender and number. 

*   **Masculine Singular:** español
*   **Feminine Singular:** española (add an -a)
*   **Masculine Plural:** españoles (change -l to -les)
*   **Feminine Plural:** españolas

*Example:*
*   el amigo español (the Spanish friend - male)
*   la amiga española (the Spanish friend - female)

### Putting Possession and Description Together

Because possessive adjectives go *before* the noun, and descriptive adjectives go *after* the noun, you can easily build complex, descriptive phrases in Spanish. 

Let's combine them using the formula: **[Possessive Adjective] + [Noun] + [Descriptive Adjective]**

*   **mi** coche **rojo** (my red car)
*   **tus** amigas **inteligentes** (your smart friends - female)
*   **nuestra** casa **grande** (our big house)
*   **su** perro **español** (his Spanish dog)

Notice how everything lines up perfectly. In the phrase *tus amigas inteligentes*, *tus* is plural to match *amigas*, and *inteligentes* is plural to match *amigas*. The descriptive adjective *inteligente* doesn't change for gender because it ends in "-e", but it takes the plural "-s" to match the noun.

## Expressing Age and Possession with Tener

To talk about what you own, you need the verb *tener*. *Tener* translates to "to have" in English. 

*   Yo tengo un coche. (I have a car.)
*   Tú tienes una casa. (You have a house.)

*Tener* is an irregular verb, meaning it doesn't follow the standard present tense conjugation patterns we covered in "Subject Pronouns and Present Tense Regular Verbs." For now, focus on memorizing these forms. We will look closer at irregular verbs in the next module.

Here is the present tense conjugation of *tener*:
*   **Yo** tengo (I have)
*   **Tú** tienes (You have - informal)
*   **Él/Ella/Usted** tiene (He/She/You have - formal)
*   **Nosotros/Nosotras** tenemos (We have)
*   **Vosotros/Vosotras** tenéis (You all have - informal, Spain)
*   **Ellos/Ellas/Ustedes** tienen (They/You all have)

### A Special Use of Tener: Age

In English, when we ask someone's age, we use the verb "to be." We say, "I *am* thirty years old." 

In Spanish, you do not use *ser* or *estar* (the two "to be" verbs). Instead, you use *tener*. Spanish approaches age as something you *have* or possess. 

To say "I am thirty years old" in Spanish, you literally say "I have thirty years."
*   **Tengo** treinta años. (I am thirty years old. / I have thirty years.)

To ask someone's age, you simply ask how many years they have:
*   ¿Cuántos años **tienes**? (How old are you? / How many years do you have?)
*   ¿Cuántos años **tiene** él? (How old is he?)

Notice the word *años*. The tilde (á) is crucial here. Without the tilde, *anos* means something entirely different (anuses). Because Spanish is a phonetic language, the tilde simply tells you to put the stress on the "a" (ah-nyos), but it also changes the meaning of the word, so spell it carefully!

### Combining Tener with Descriptions

Now you can combine everything you've learned to introduce yourself, state your age, and describe what you have. 

Let's look at a realistic scenario. Imagine you are meeting a new language exchange partner online. You want to tell them your age, that you have a dog, and describe your dog.

1.  **State your age:** Tengo veinticinco años. (I am twenty-five years old.)
2.  **Say what you have:** Tengo un perro. (I have a dog.)
3.  **Describe what you have:** Mi perro es pequeño y blanco. (My dog is small and white. *Note: We use 'es' here because we are describing an inherent characteristic of the dog, a concept covered in Chapter 5.*)

Let's try another one, describing a friend's family:
*   "Mi amiga tiene dos coches." (My friend has two cars.)
*   "Sus coches son rápidos y rojos." (Her cars are fast and red.)

Notice the agreement in that second sentence. *Coches* is masculine plural. Therefore, *rojos* (which ends in -o) must be masculine plural, and *rápidos* must be masculine plural. Even though *su* doesn't change for gender, the descriptive adjectives at the end of the sentence do all the heavy lifting for agreement.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Possessive adjectives** show who owns an object. They are placed *before* the noun (e.g., *mi casa*).
*   Possessive adjectives must match the **number** (singular/plural) of the object owned. Only *nuestro* and *vuestro* also change to match the **gender** (masculine/feminine) of the object.
*   **Su** and **sus** can mean his, her, its, your (formal), or their. Rely on context to understand the meaning, or use a phrase like *de él* or *de ella* to clarify.
*   **Descriptive adjectives** describe nouns. In Spanish, they are normally placed *after* the noun (e.g., *la casa roja*).
*   Descriptive adjectives must **agree in gender and number** with the noun they modify. Adjectives ending in "-o" have four forms (*-o, -a, -os, -as*). Adjectives ending in "-e" or a consonant usually only change for number (*-s* or *-es*).
*   Use the verb **tener** to express possession ("I have a book" = *Tengo un libro*).
*   Use **tener** to express age ("I am 20 years old" = *Tengo veinte años*). Do not use *ser* or *estar* for age.

modules.title: High-Frequency Irregular Verbs
modules.content:
## The Irregular Verbs You Need Every Day

Imagine you wake up in a hotel room in Madrid. Your throat is dry, your stomach is rumbling, and you need to ask the front desk where to find a café. You want to say: "I am thirsty, I am hungry, and I want to go to a restaurant." 

If you try to translate this word-for-word from English using the regular verb rules we covered in Chapter 3, you will hit a wall. In Spanish, you don't say "I have hunger" or "I am thirst"—you use specific expressions built around the verb *tener* (to have). You also need the verb *querer* (to want) to express your desires, and the verb *ir* (to go) to explain your destination.

These are high-frequency verbs—the workhorses of everyday conversation. But there is a catch: they are irregular. 

In Chapter 3, we learned that regular verbs follow predictable patterns based on their endings (-ar, -er, -ir). Irregular verbs, however, refuse to follow those rules. They change their spelling in their own unique ways. While this sounds intimidating, these irregularities exist for a reason: they make the verbs easier to pronounce. Because Spanish is a highly phonetic language, these spelling changes keep the natural stress and sounds of the words consistent.

In this chapter, we will master the four most essential irregular verbs—*tener*, *querer*, *hacer*, and *ir*—and learn how to use them to express physical states, make plans, and talk about the near future.

## Jargon Check: Stem and Root

Before we dive into conjugations, we need to define two quick grammatical terms: **stem** and **root**. 

If you take a regular verb like *hablar* (to speak) and remove the -ar ending, you are left with the stem: *habl-*. You then add your endings (like -o, -as, -a) to this stem. 

Sometimes, textbooks use the term **root** to mean the exact same thing as the stem. For the purposes of this chapter, if you see "stem" or "root," just remember: it is the core part of the verb before you add the ending that tells you *who* is doing the action.

## The Verb "Tener" (To Have)

In English, the verb "to have" is mostly used to show possession (I have a cat) or to form compound tenses (I have eaten). In Spanish, *tener* does both of those things, but it also does much more. 

*Tener* is a **stem-changing verb**. This means that instead of just changing its endings like a regular verb, the vowel in the stem also changes. For *tener*, the **e** changes to **ie**. 

Notice, however, that this change only happens in the *yo*, *tú*, *él/ella/usted*, and *ellos/ellas/ustedes* forms. The *nosotros* and *vosotros* forms keep the original **e**. This is a universal rule in Spanish: stem-changing verbs never change their stem in the *nosotros* and *vosotros* forms.

Here is the present tense conjugation of *tener*:

*   **Yo tengo** (I have)
*   **Tú tienes** (You have)
*   **Él/Ella/Usted tiene** (He/She/You formal have)
*   **Nosotros/Nosotras tenemos** (We have)
*   **Vosotros/Vosotras tenéis** (You all have)
